sections.paragraph:
σύντονος, ον (‘stretched tight, intense, vehement’ Trag. et al.; Philo, Joseph.) the neut. as subst. τὸ σύντονον intense desire, zeal (Philo, Leg. ad Gai. τὸ ς. τῆς σπουδῆς) ὑμῶν τὸ σύντονον τῆς ἀληθείας your intense desire for the truth IPol 7:3.—The neut. of the comp. as adv. (Aristot., Pol. 5, 8, 2; Plut., Cato Maj. 349 [21, 5]; Jos., Bell. 1, 274; 3, 13) συντονώτερον γράφειν write more sharply ITr 3:3.—DELG s.v. τείνω.

Trag.

Trag. = writer (s) of tragedy

trag. = tragedy, writer (s) of tragedy

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Joseph Joseph This abbr. used when no specific texts are cited (s. Jos.); I a.d.—List 5

neut. neut. = neuter (used when “n.” would be ambiguous)

subst. subst. = substantive(ly)

IPol IPol = Ignatius to Polycarp—List 1

comp. comp. = comparative(ly)

adv. adv. = adverb, adverbially

Aristot Aristot , various works, IV b.c.—List 5

Pol Pol = Polycarp to the Philippians—List 1

Plut Plut , I–II a.d.—List 5

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

ITr ITr = Ignatius to the Trallians—List 1

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 976
